title: Resonant Coupling and the Non-Phononic Flat Band in Amorphous Solids
abstract: Recent experiments and simulations provide compelling evidence for the emergence of a non-phononic flat band in the dynamical structure factor of two- and three-dimensional amorphous solids. This feature has been suggested to be connected to the excess in the reduced vibrational density of states of glasses, commonly known as the boson peak, and displays several apparently universal characteristics. First, it is nearly dispersionless, with an energy close to the boson-peak frequency. Second, its intensity is negligible below a critical wave vector of the order of the first diffraction peak. Third, its reduced intensity exhibits a strong correlation with the static structure factor. Here, we revisit the resonant-coupling model, a single-mode harmonic realization of the soft-potential scenario in which acoustic phonons interact with single frequency quasi-localized vibrations. We show that this minimal framework naturally reproduces the main features of the observed flat band and clarifies its connection to the boson peak.
summary: This work investigates the non-phononic flat band in amorphous solids by developing a resonant-coupling model. The model posits that the flat band arises from the resonant interaction between acoustic phonons and localized vibrational modes (QLVs). This framework successfully explains the observed spectral features and links the flat band's energy directly to the characteristic Boson Peak frequency.
main_problem: The paper addresses the physical origin and nature of the non-phononic flat band observed in the dynamical structure factor of amorphous solids.
main_result: A resonant-coupling model successfully reproduces the key features of the flat band, establishing its connection to the boson peak via coupling between acoustic phonons and quasi-localized vibrations.
method: The authors employ a resonant-coupling model derived from a soft-potential scenario, analyzing the renormalized phonon Green's function.
model_or_system: The system is two- and three-dimensional amorphous solids. The model involves coupling acoustic phonons (phononic modes) with single-frequency quasi-localized vibrations (QLVs) via a resonant interaction term.
key_observables: Dynamical structure factor $S(q, \omega)$, the non-phononic flat band signal, and the Boson Peak (BP) in the vibrational density of states.
important_parameters: The critical wave vector $q^*$ (related to the first diffraction peak) and the coupling strength $g(q)$ which is hypothesized to scale with the static structure factor $S(q)$.
main_assumptions: The model assumes a single QLV frequency and damping, and the resonant coupling framework is fundamentally macroscopic and phenomenological.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the flat band signal relative to the phononic peak, show the correlation between flat-band intensity and $S(q)$, and demonstrate the emergence of the flat band at the QLV frequency.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem using experimental observations, develops the resonant-coupling model (RCM) by modifying the phonon Green's function, derives the resulting flat-band intensity, and compares the model's predictions against known structural correlations.
why_interesting: The concept of resonant coupling between propagating modes (phonons) and localized excitations (QLVs) is relevant to understanding energy dissipation and mode hybridization in complex, disordered quantum systems, which has parallels in open quantum systems theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: The emergence of the non-phononic flat band in the dynamic structure factor of amorphous solids: the example of metallic glasses. Constant-q cuts of the longitudinal dynamical structure factor SL(q, ω) as a function of frequency ω for a simulated three-dimensional metallic glass. The wave vector q increases from the top-left panel to the bottom-right panel; q = 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.9, 1, 1.1, 1.7, 1.8, 1.9, 2.5, 2.6, 2.7, 3.2, 3.3, 3.4. The red dashed arrow indicates the dispersing phononic peak, while the filled black arrow marks the non-phononic peak associated with the flat band. The data are reproduced with permission from Ref. [10].

FIG. 2: Example of the hybridized excitation spec- trum. Top: Density map of the dynamic structure factor S(q, ω) and dispersion relation of the modes (black dashed curves) showing the avoided crossing and frequency splitting around qres = 1. Bottom: dynamic structure factor at fixed q = 1.5 (red vertical dashed line in top panel), displaying the characteristic two-peak structure, which is absent in the limit of vanishing coupling to QLVs (black dashed curve). Parameters: ωLV = kBT = M = 1 with Ω(q) = q, γ = 0.1, Γ(q) = 0.2q2 and g(q) = 0.1q2. The power-law scalings of Γ(q), g(q) do not carry any specific physical meaning at this stage.

FIG. 3: Resonant coupling and flat-band intensity. Ex- ample of a resonant coupling g(q) that vanishes below a crit- ical wavevector q∗≈2.5 (inset). The corresponding density map of S(q, ω) displays a flat-band signal emerging only for q > q∗. Here, M = kBT = 1, Ω(q) = q, Γ(q) = 0.2q2, γ = 0.15, and ωQLV = 0.8 (horizontal black dashed line).

FIG. 4: Static structure factor and flat-band intensity. A representative example in which the normalized flat-band intensity, f(q)/q2, closely follows the trend of an artificial static structure factor S(q) (orange dashed line), introduced in the model through the simplified choice g(q) ∝S(q). The remaining parameters are set to kBT = M = 1, γ = 0.1, Ω(q) = 0.5 q, Γ(q) = 5 × 10−4 q2, and ωQLV = 10. For clarity of visualization, the black curve is rescaled by an appropriate constant.

FIG. 5: Anti-correlation between flat-band dispersion and resonant coupling/static structure factor. Top panel: Illustration of the anti-correlation between the flat- band dispersion (color background and solid blue line) and the resonant coupling g(q) (black dashed line), modeled here as an oscillatory function to mimic the behavior of a realistic static structure factor S(q). For clarity, the amplitude of g(q) is rescaled by a factor of 30. The others parameters used in this figure are: Γ = 10−3q2, kB = T = M = 1, γ = 0.01, ωQLV = 2, while the long-wavelength phonon dispersion is taken as Ω(q) = 3q + . . .
